posts.html:
The Daily Californian is an independent, student-run newspaper published by the Independent Berkeley Students Publishing Company, Inc. The Daily Californian covers both the UC Berkeley campus and the city of Berkeley.

A board of directors composed of Daily Cal alumni, UC Berkeley professors and working news media professionals oversees long-term fiscal management for the newspaper. The board of directors has no input or control over the Daily Cal’s editorial policy or newsroom operations.

In addition, another external board, the Daily Californian Education Foundation, oversees projects for the newspaper staff’s training and fundraising for the Daily Cal’s continual growth. The Education Foundation, which consists mostly of Daily Cal alumni, also has no influence on editorial content or policy.

The Daily Californian Alumni Association is one the largest and most active alumni associations of any college newspaper. A chartered club of the California Alumni Association, the alumni association holds regular reunions and receptions across the country. Alumni are established and leading journalists in news organizations such as The Los Angeles Times, The New York Times, People magazine, The Arizona Republic, The San Francisco Chronicle, The San Francisco Examiner, Sports Illustrated, NBC News, USA Today and The Washington Post.

History

Established in 1871, The Daily Californian is one of the oldest newspapers on the West Coast and one of the oldest college newspapers in the country.

The Daily Californian began as The University Echo shortly after the founding of the University of California. This publication was born out of the College Echo, published by the Durant Rhetorical Society at the College of California. 

In 1874, The University Echo and the Neolean Review merged into The Berkeleyan. The publication took a hiatus from 1888 to 1892, beginning a new series once again in 1893. 

The name was changed to The Californian and then later The Daily Californian in 1897. 

In 1971, the Daily Cal’s editors published a front-page editorial, exhorting protesters to “take back the park.” After widespread criticism, the editors tried to keep the ensuing confrontation peaceful, but when the demonstration turned violent, the university’s Publisher’s Board tried to fire the two editors responsible for the editorial. The other editors quit in protest, and the whole editorial board regrouped and continued to put out the newspaper on its own.

After a series of negotiations with university administrators, the editors agreed to move off campus and operate the paper independently as a nonprofit organization, continuing to use the name The Daily Californian under a special licensing agreement with the UC Board of Regents. The university agreed not to publish or sponsor any other newspaper whose primary focus was to cover university news and even contributed some money to assist in reestablishing the paper.

After spending years off campus at various Southside locations, the Daily Cal moved back on campus in 1994. 

In 1996, the Daily Cal launched the “Sex on Tuesday” column, the first regular college newspaper sex column. 

The Daily Cal cut Wednesday print in 2009 opting to only print 4 days a week. 

In summer 2012, the Daily Cal moved out of Eshleman Hall after the announcement that the building would be demolished. The new office of the student news outlet is where the Daily Cal now resides — 2483 Hearst Ave. on the Northside of Berkeley. 

During that same year, the Daily Cal also had a referendum on the ASUC general election ballot. The V.O.I.C.E. Initiative granted the Daily Cal with a new source of funding through $2 from all UC Berkeley students every semester. When the fee expired in 2017, a $2.50 semesterly fee took effect through the Ink Initiative, which the student body approved in spring 2016. 

In 2020, the Daily Cal cut print to only one day a week due to the COVID-19 pandemic; this change was made permanent in 2022.  

Following the Ink Initiative’s expiration in 2022, the Save the Daily Cal Initiative was passed. This new initiative raised the fee to $6 per student per semester during the academic year and $2.50 per student during the summer, applied to both undergraduate and graduate students. The Save the Daily Cal Initiative will run until summer 2027.

Now, The Daily Californian prints weekly Thursdays, with content being published online everyday. The Daily Cal prizes its independence and remains the newspaper of record for the city of Berkeley and the student newspaper for UC Berkeley. The newspaper’s content is produced entirely by current or recently-graduated UC Berkeley students. 

The Daily Cal has been recognized with awards by numerous organizations including the California College Media Association, The Associated College Press, California News Publishers Association, the Society of Professional Journalists and more. 

Publishing

The Daily Cal is published online every day and in print on Thursday by The Independent Berkeley Student Publishing Company Inc., or IBSPC. The nonprofit IBSPC serves to support an editorially independent newsroom run by UC Berkeley students.

This publication is not an official publication of the University of California but is published by an independent corporation using the name “The Daily Californian” pursuant to a license granted by the Regents of the University of California.

Advertisements appearing in The Daily Californian reflect the views of the advertisers only. They are not an expression of editorial opinion or the views of staff members. Opinions expressed in The Daily Californian by editors or columnists regarding candidates for political office or legislation are those of the editors or columnists and are not those of the Independent Berkeley Student Publishing Company Inc. Unsigned editorials are the collective opinion of the Editorial Board.

Copyright 2025. All rights reserved. Reproduction in any form (whether in whole or in part) without written permission from the editor is strictly prohibited.

Interactive Edition

Receive The Daily Californian every day, right in your inbox. Every issue we publish in print is now simultaneously published into a web-friendly mode that duplicates the experience of reading our printed edition. Click here to see our interactive edition.

Subscriptions

Subscribe to The Daily Californian to receive outstanding coverage of the UC Berkeley community. To begin a subscription, please fill out the form (pdf below), enclose payment (check or credit card information), and return it to The Daily Californian. Issues are mailed every two weeks. The rates are $50 for 6 months, $90 for a year, and $165 for 2 years. If you have any questions about subscriptions, please call The Daily Californian at 510-548-8300 or e-mail subscription@dailycal.org. Thank you for your support!

posts.html:
UC Berkeley is launching the Nancy Pelosi Institute, which aims to serve as a nonpartisan hub for “strengthening American democracy.” Pelosi, Speaker Emerita of the House and the Representative for California’s 11th Congressional District, will co-teach a course on Congress with political science professor Eric Schickler.

Launching in January 2027, the Nancy Pelosi Institute, or NPI, will encompass faculty research, teaching undergraduate courses and a visiting fellows program within the campus’s political science department.

According to UC Berkeley News, the NPI has received more than $35 million in philanthropic commitments, primarily earmarked for a permanent endowment, with a fundraising goal of $50 million to establish the institute as a national leader in research and fellowships.

The NPI is aiming to prepare UC Berkeley undergraduate students for public service, advocacy and future leadership, according to the Monday press release. It also plans to “bring high-profile national and world leaders for a semester” and host an annual nonpartisan forum.

Scott Straus, chair of the political science department, said in a press conference the department will be actively involved in managing the NPI. According to Straus, the institute’s core curriculum focus will be a public leadership track, with an overview course on national challenges and potential solutions.

“As political scientists, fundamentally, we are asking questions about politics and we are using evidence to answer those questions,” Straus said at the press conference. “We are committed to nonpartisan teaching in the classroom … Our instruction is designed to help people think critically and analytically about politics.”

Faculty research at the institute will involve finding solutions to national issues, including climate change, criminal justice reform and how AI can “enable instead of undermine” democracy, according to the press release.

Political science professor Gabriel Lenz said he is “thrilled” that the Nancy Pelosi Institute will reside at UC Berkeley, especially considering Pelosi’s success as a public servant. Although Lenz is not directly involved with the NPI, he noted that almost all American politics faculty have been involved in some capacity or have projects they hope to take on.

“This is kind of the next best thing to a presidential library,” Lenz said. “The other component is fundraising. When someone prominent steps down and then sets up an institute like this where their papers will be kept … it’s an opportunity to raise funds for all these programs. You just don’t get these kinds of opportunities very often.”

In the spring of next year, the Bancroft Library is slated to host an exhibit on Pelosi’s life and career in the House of Representatives. Pelosi was the first female Speaker of the House, served as chair of the California Democratic Party and has held her position in the House of Representatives since 1987. She is not running for reelection this year.

“UC Berkeley has a long, proud history of challenging the status quo and producing leaders who run toward the greatest challenges of our time,” Pelosi said in a press release. “I am honored to partner with this exceptional community of scholars and students so we can equip the next generation with the tools they need to strengthen our democratic institutions and forge a future that serves the public good.”

The NPI is aiming to prepare UC Berkeley undergraduate students for public service, advocacy and future leadership, according to the Monday press release. It also plans to “bring high-profile national and world leaders for a semester” and host an annual nonpartisan forum.

Scott Straus, chair of the political science department, said in a press conference the department will be actively involved in managing the NPI. According to Straus, the institute’s core curriculum focus will be a public leadership track, with an overview course on national challenges and potential solutions.

“As political scientists, fundamentally, we are asking questions about politics and we are using evidence to answer those questions,” Straus said at the press conference. “We are committed to nonpartisan teaching in the classroom … Our instruction is designed to help people think critically and analytically about politics.”

Faculty research at the institute will involve finding solutions to national issues, including climate change, criminal justice reform and how AI can “enable instead of undermine” democracy, according to the press release.

Political science professor Gabriel Lenz said he is “thrilled” that the Nancy Pelosi Institute will reside at UC Berkeley, especially considering Pelosi’s success as a public servant. Although Lenz is not directly involved with the NPI, he noted that almost all American politics faculty have been involved in some capacity or have projects they hope to take on.

“This is kind of the next best thing to a presidential library,” Lenz said. “The other component is fundraising. When someone prominent steps down and then sets up an institute like this where their papers will be kept … it’s an opportunity to raise funds for all these programs. You just don’t get these kinds of opportunities very often.”

In the spring of next year, the Bancroft Library is slated to host an exhibit on Pelosi’s life and career in the House of Representatives. Pelosi was the first female Speaker of the House, served as chair of the California Democratic Party and has held her position in the House of Representatives since 1987. She is not running for reelection this year.

“UC Berkeley has a long, proud history of challenging the status quo and producing leaders who run toward the greatest challenges of our time,” Pelosi said in a press release. “I am honored to partner with this exceptional community of scholars and students so we can equip the next generation with the tools they need to strengthen our democratic institutions and forge a future that serves the public good.”

posts.html:
Gov. Gavin Newsom approved California’s state budget for the fiscal year 2026-27, which approves $355 billion in spending, despite the Legislative Analyst’s Office’s May projection of a $16.9 billion deficit. The budget protects UC funding but cuts healthcare coverage and funding for some student financial aid programs as Newsom aims to balance state finances.

The cuts reduce funding for the Middle Class Scholarship, aimed at low- to middle-income UC and CSU students. While it previously covered up to 35% of eligible students’ remaining cost of attendance after other aid, it will cover only 17.5% of the remaining cost of attendance for the 2026-27 school year under the new plan. This is expected to reduce the cost of the program from $1.1 billion to $531 million.

The budget designates a total of approximately $5.4 billion in funding, honoring Newsom’s 2022 compact with the UC and CSU systems to annually increase their funding by 5% over the span of five years.

“None of these are easy choices, but given the federal cuts and concerns about revenues for the California budget, they are the kinds of hard choices that legislators face,” said Henry E. Brady, a professor of political science and public policy, in an email. “The decision to protect higher education comes after decades of cuts and a feeling that the state’s UC’s and CSU’s are getting at the end of their ability to perform given the low-level of state support.”

According to Newsom’s press release following the May revision, the budget is projected to eliminate the state’s deficit for the next two budget years and prevent a structural deficit through July 2028. His office’s prediction differs, however, from the Legislative Analyst’s Office projection of a $16.9 billion operating deficit in May 2026.

State Department of Finance spokesperson H.D. Palmer noted that funding for the Cal Grant Program, a student financial aid program that also serves low- and middle-income students, will see an estimated increase of approximately $300 million compared to funding from the fiscal year 2025-26.

“The 7% increase from the state goes a long way toward fulfilling the compact and providing the funding necessary to educate and invest in our students,” said UC President James B. Milliken. “This is especially important given the sustained attacks against UC and funding cuts by the federal government over the past 18 months.”

The budget also delays Medi-Cal reductions for non-citizens in response to federal government decisions to reduce Health and Human Services programs or change eligibility requirements, according to Palmer. Reductions are still set to be enacted in fiscal year 2027-28.

Brady said healthcare and education allocations pose difficulties for legislators, who must deal with “powerful constituencies” and balance future-focused education goals with more immediate challenges such as illness.

“To the greatest extent possible, both the Governor and the Legislature have strived to maintain coverage for those Californians who are currently receiving these services,” Palmer wrote in an email.

posts.html:
A study released by UC Berkeley’s Center for Studies in Higher Education found a correlation between the release of ChatGPT in 2022 and an increased share of A grades for classes with take-home assignments at a selective public university in Texas. However, Berkeleytime data collected by The Daily Californian found no equivalent trends at UC Berkeley.

Between 2019 and spring 2026, A’s at UC Berkeley consistently stayed between 30% and 35%, with a dip during the COVID-19 pandemic likely due to increased pass/no pass courses.

The study, authored by Igor Chirikov, a senior researcher and director of the Student Experience in the Research University Consortium at the Center for Studies in Higher Education, analyzed 500,000 grades from 2018-25 and found that the share of A grades increased by 13 percentage points at the Texas university.

“The pattern shows grades rising mainly where students can use AI on unsupervised take-home work,” Chirikov said in an email. “This strongly suggests that AI is substituting for student effort rather than improving underlying skills.”

While the study found that grades have been increasing in classes with lots of take-home assignments, grades across UC Berkeley have remained relatively stable.

The Daily Cal scraped more than 30,000 course grades beginning in the summer of 2019 from Berkeleytime to assess grade trends at UC Berkeley compared to the university in the study.

Nevertheless, professors across departments on campus have been feeling and responding to the challenges that AI presents to their classrooms.

Josh Hug, a professor in the electrical engineering and computer sciences department, has found that the amount of AI-generated work submitted by students has increased considerably, but said grades have decreased.

In EECS and computer science courses, some classes’ failing grades have more than doubled in the spring 2026 semester. Professors attributed the increase to an increased reliance on AI, a lack of math preparedness and understaffing within classes.

“The reason is that they’re still getting full scores on the assignments, but they’re doing much worse on exams,” Hug said.

To adapt to AI, Hug has been incorporating frequent in-class quizzes, oral check-ins and “diagnostic questions” on exams.

Hug reuses questions from past exams and compares the performance between the two groups of students.

“I have found that students do far worse on these problems than they used to, which is my biggest piece of evidence that they don’t quite know what they’re doing in the way they used to,” Hug said.

History professor Mark Brilliant has run into similar challenges, with students especially struggling with writing.

“Writing is the biggest challenge,” Brilliant said. “Nothing substitutes for writing as a way to develop one’s own voice — one’s intellectual voice.”

In the spring 2026 semester, Brilliant integrated Rumi into his assignments, a platform that allows professors to analyze keystrokes, copy and paste history and potential use of large language models. As a result, he was able to analyze the writing process of students and look for signs of AI.

Michael Mark Cohen, a professor in the American studies and African American studies departments, has similar concerns about students submitting AI-generated papers.

Similar to Hug, Cohen has returned to having more in-person assignments and requirements such as tracking attendance, in-class exams and presentations.

Cohen expressed frustration with the time it takes for both him and his GSIs to sift through papers that use AI. As a result, he has failed “twice as many people” as he did before.

“If you want to cheat, then in the end you’re cheating yourself,” Cohen said. “At some point somewhere down the line, somebody’s going to realize that you paid for a very expensive education and didn’t learn anything.”

posts.html:
The UC system released new data Wednesday that argues for the economic benefits of a UC degree by looking at affordability, return on investment and annual earnings.

This comes in the wake of growing suspicion of higher education, with an October 2025 Pew Research Center survey finding that seven in 10 Americans believe that the higher education system in the United States is headed in the “wrong direction.”

“Many Americans are questioning the value of a college degree,” said UC President James B. Milliken in a press release. “We have a clear answer: A UC degree is not only worth it — it changes lives and entire communities for the better.”

However, according to LinkedIn’s analysis of 2022-24 bachelor’s degree graduates, job hiring is still a struggle — it remains 5% below last year and more than 20% below pre-pandemic levels. LinkedIn’s analysis noted that job competition has intensified, with applicants per role “roughly doubling since 2022.”

Based on data from students who entered the UC system between 2008-09, the study found that 15 years after enrollment, UC bachelor’s degree recipients had more than $600,000 in earnings, compared with high school graduates at the same age.

The study accounted for the costs of getting a UC degree, including tuition and the loss of potential wages immediately after high school. The study also claimed that the net value added of a UC degree increased after 2010.

“Among young adults, those with at least a bachelor’s degree are more likely to be employed than those who only have a high school education,” said Richard Fry, a senior economist with Pew Research Center, in an email. “Among those working, college graduates are more likely to work full-time, full-year than high school graduates.”

UC undergraduate alumni break even on their educational investment — which includes the remaining cost of tuition, fees, books and more, after financial aid — approximately six years after initial enrollment, the UC data claimed.

More than 60% of UC undergraduates graduate debt-free, according to UCOP spokesperson Omar Rodriguez.

“For years, you go through education and then four years of college and the lack of set guidance can be daunting to say the least,” said 2026 campus alumnus William Lopez, a state Assembly legislative and communications intern, in an email. “But what is always reassuring is to remember that it’s part of the process — figuring out what you’re passionate about and following through to see where it takes you.”

posts.html:
The city of Berkeley’s budget, adopted June 23, made a number of critical cuts to almost every sector of city services while continuing to fund affordable housing projects that have previously faced cuts.

The budget cuts were made to address the $30 million budget deficit the city faced last year. However, with housing being a top issue for many during public comment, the proposed reallocation of affordable housing development funding proved controversial.

After significant deliberation at the City Council, the council unanimously approved the budget proposed by Mayor Adena Ishii. Ishii’s budget allocated $3.8 million toward the Small Sites Program, or SSP, an increase from the $2.5 million it was given before the new budget.

SSP helps fund renovations of multifamily buildings to turn them into permanent, affordable housing, preventing displacement. The program is run through the city’s Housing Trust Fund and is often used by land trusts and cooperative housing groups.

District 1 Councilmember Rashi Kesarwani proposed an amendment to the city budget that would have reallocated the SSP funding toward the development of new affordable housing builds instead of restoration projects.

This became a controversial topic in public comment, with many community members speaking out against Kesarwani’s reallocations.

One such community member was Leah Simon-Weisberg, a resident of District 2, housing attorney and executive director at the California Center for Movement Legal Services. She argues for the importance of the type of community-led affordable housing that can be built with the SSP funding.

“There’s just this deep respect for the tenants, and they feel seen; they get to participate,” Simon-Weisberg said. “It’s not a welfare model (where) somehow the people who are powerful get to make all the decisions … it just really makes a difference if where you live, you feel respect. You feel like you have a say.”

Simon-Weisberg also noted that SSP funding has faced cuts in recent years, such as in June 2025, when the council voted to cut $1.4 million from the program.

She credits part of this year’s success in maintaining the SSP funding to a great effort of community organizing, saying this year “the community came well prepared” to push back against Kesarwani’s proposal.

One organization that advocated for SSP funding was the Bay Area Community Land Trust. It worked with and supported SSP since its founding, according to the organizational director, Matt Gustafson.

Gustafson agreed that the success of maintaining SSP funding is because of community organizing and pushback, adding, “You don’t have affordable housing dollars in this city if you don’t have that coalition of organized housing people.”

Gustafson argued that preservation, production and protection of tenants are all important parts of affordable housing. He said they are advocating for some of the affordable housing funding to go toward small site preservation, and that hundreds of millions of dollars already go toward production.

“There was a tremendous amount of community support for small sites for housing preservation this year in the budget,” Gustafson said. “May the record reflect that the people of Berkeley want small sites and believe housing preservation is an important value for our city.”

Kesarwani and city communications did not respond to requests for comment as of press time. Ishii was unable to provide comment by press time.

posts.html:
Walk into Serotonin Scoops, and you’ll find a bright display of flavors, from smooth chocolate to tropical mango. The shop offers a variety of ice cream choices, with one twist: up to 34 grams of protein.

Located in the Epicurious Garden on 1511 Shattuck Ave., Serotonin Scoops contains low-calorie, high-protein ice cream, making for the perfect healthy treat. The store opened June 19, offering flavors such as saffron, pistachio and cardamom, alongside classics such as vanilla and strawberry.

The shop was created by Jay Desai, who, after being diagnosed with diabetes, began looking for a way to make desserts healthy, without compromising on taste or texture.

Desai later partnered with microbiologist Reza Sadat, who worked with him to fulfill Desai’s ice cream dreams.

After years of work, Desai and Sadat reached their goal, making ice cream that had no added sugar, artificial sweeteners or sugar alcohols, instead sweetening it with their blend of monk fruit and allulose. The ice cream is additionally GLP-1-friendly and made with added fiber and prebiotics.

“After having dessert, there’s always a kind of guilt behind your mind, saying you had a lot of sugar, a lot of calories,” said Sandesh Khanal, manager and operating partner.

Serotonin Scoops plans to change that by providing a dessert that’s both nutritious and sweet. The key, according to the founders, was not to focus on making healthy or diet ice cream. Instead, they centered around taste, making it their top priority, and implemented healthier, plant-based ingredients.

“We’re going to have something that’s a very healthy, guilt-free dessert,” Khanal said.

Rohit Singh, owner of Berkeley’s Breads of India and Home Made Cafe and one of the founders, was brought in by Desai to provide insight on a Berkeley location.

Part of what made Berkeley the ideal setting for the shop was the city’s deep love for and appreciation for all kinds of food, according to a statement from Serotonin Scoops. Ingrained in Berkeley, besides the timeless classics, are also restaurants and stores offering more innovative approaches.

“Berkeley is a place where people are open-minded, where before they discard anything, they try it. For me, it’s about the approval of Berkeley people,” Singh said. “If we can establish ourselves in Berkeley, we’ll have more confidence to go to other cities.”

While some people were skeptical about the high-protein, low-calorie ice cream offered at Serotonin Scoops, the owners found that their opinions often changed after trying the ice cream.

“What we have done here is whenever I get such comments, I have some other outsourced ice cream and I just let them test both our ice cream and the other ice cream, like a blind test, and people usually prefer our ice cream,” Khanal said. “We have done that for a minimum of 20 people already here, and I think 17 to 18 people chose ours.”

Bridging together nutrition and flavor, Serotonin Scoops elevated a timeless summer staple into a pleasurable, guilt-free experience, all the while preserving every ounce of taste and texture.

posts.html:
The 2026 World Cup has set a new attendance record, with more than 3.6 million fans attending the tournament. Games are being played across North America, which gives fans many opportunities to watch and support their country. I was able to go to the Paraguay vs. Australia match that took place at the San Francisco Bay Area Stadium on June 25, and this is what I would recommend when going to a World Cup game.

When arriving, make sure to give yourself time to get parking and go through the long security lines. There are tens of thousands of fans in attendance and very few entrances to the stadium, so I would allot two hours of time to pass through security, take photos, get food and go to the bathroom before the game begins.

After going through security but before you enter the stadium, there are many popups with brands that sponsor the World Cup, such as Coca-Cola and Home Depot, where fans can take photos. I enjoyed the Home Depot popup because it had a background with a list of each group in the group stage, and I was able to take a picture in front of the stand. There are also lots of trucks with official FIFA merchandise. This is where you can buy T-shirts, hats and soccer balls with World Cup or individual team branding. The stadiums are loud with live music and fans cheering, excited to watch their country play. There are also many people in lime green and purple T-shirts who hold signs; these are the staff who can help guide you to your seats and answer any questions.

Once you enter the stadium, there are places to purchase food and drinks. There are long lines, but you can buy hotdogs, chips, nachos or other refreshments that the stadium offers. With about 30 minutes before the game, I would recommend that you find your seats because you’ll get to watch the teams walk out and listen to each country’s national anthem. FIFA brings out the flags of each country, and listening to all of the fans sing their national anthem with pride was one of my favorite parts of the night. The announcers also list the starting lineups, so this allows you to understand the formation that each team will play, giving you background information before the match starts.

Looking around the stadium, each section is split by color. In my case, one side was fully yellow, representing Australia, and those fans held blow up kangaroos. For Paraguay, many of the fans wore red and white, held up massive signs and played drums the entire match. For both teams, many fans wore jerseys and scarves with either their country’s name or nickname. Australia’s nickname, for instance, is the Socceroos. Each section of the stadium was filled with energy and pride for their country, and fans of all ages attended in support.

On screen in the middle of the game there were live updates from the other game in their bracket. That day, the goals from Türkiye and the United States were displayed.

Ten seconds before kickoff, there is a countdown on the screens all around the stadium, marking a thrilling start to the game.

When the game starts, fans sing and play music, sharing their support for their country. During the middle of the Paraguay vs. Australia game, the entire stadium did a massive arm wave, signalling unison and joy.

Halftime is the perfect time to refill your snacks and go to the restroom, but be mindful of the massive lines. When arriving back to your seats, this is a great time to try and get on the Jumbotron. The stadium had music playing and many fans were dancing, trying to capture the attention of the camera. At the start of the second half, there is another 10-second countdown, which sparks excitement throughout the stadium again.

Once the game is over, the team that continues in the tournament celebrates by running and hugging each other, and their fans erupt in cheers. In my case, the Australian team members ran to each other, ecstatic because they had moved onto the round of 32. The highlights of the game are played on screens around the stadium which can be viewed by fans leaving. The exits can be very crowded, so I recommend staying close to the people that you are with to ensure no one is pulled away from the group.

My experience watching the Australia vs. Paraguay game felt surreal, and hearing the beating of the drums from the Paraguay fans and laughs from the Australia fans made the stadium feel alive. If you have the opportunity to watch a World Cup game in person, you will not be disappointed.

posts.html:
U.S. News & World Report 2026-27 has ranked UC Berkeley as the No. 1 public university in the U.S., No. 4 U.S. university in the global rankings and No. 7 university in the world. This should come as no surprise to us, Golden Bears — UC Berkeley’s groundbreaking research and academic excellence continue to change the world and attest to its world-class rankings. 

You may not know that you’re passing the birthplaces of trailblazing discoveries, revolutionary inventions and world-changing collaborations on your walk to class every day. Chances are, the lecture hall, professor’s office or familiar laboratory part of your regular routine is also home to a discovery that changed the world. This guide will take you to some of those places.

Periodic table elements

Welcome to the University of Californium, Berkelium! Did you know that UC Berkeley has discovered more periodic table elements than any other university in the world? The Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory is home to this record with the discovery of 16 elements. Perhaps the most notable of these is plutonium, discovered and isolated by Glenn Seaborg in Room 307 of Gilman Hall — a room now designated as a National Historic Landmark. If you’re particularly proud of our periodic table achievements, pick up some berkelium merchandise, and every time you wear it, remember that you attend one of the best universities in the world.

CRISPR gene editing

One thing that makes going to a world-class university so special is that you never know if the meeting happening next to you at a cafe is about something trivial or the next revolutionary breakthrough. Nobel laureate Jennifer Doudna is the perfect example of this — famously meeting at the now temporarily closed Free Speech Movement Cafe to discuss the beginnings of what would later become her Nobel Prize-winning CRISPR research. Alongside collaborator Emmanuelle Charpentier, Doudna pioneered this gene-editing technology, which allows scientists to cut, edit and rewrite DNA with precision. Through her groundbreaking research done at the very familiar Stanley Hall, CRISPR has since unlocked new possibilities in treating genetic disorders, fighting cancer and restoring sight. Doudna’s impact at UC Berkeley did not stop there, as she went on to found the Innovative Genomics Institute right here on campus, further advancing the life-changing work that defines our university.

Atomic bomb

Could you imagine working with your professor to create something as monumental as the atomic bomb? That’s exactly what J. Robert Oppenheimer did with his students while working as a physics professor at UC Berkeley. Oppenheimer gathered his students and peers in his office, which is now the Physics South building, to lay the theoretical groundwork for what would become one of the most consequential scientific endeavors in history. The same building you may walk past on your way to class every day is a reminder of the brilliant and innovative minds that UC Berkeley attracts — and the historical legacy that earned us our No. 1 ranking.

I hope this guide showed you that the next world-changing discovery can be right beneath your feet. The buildings you walk past, the cafes you study in and the rooms you visit during office hours are the same spaces that produced Nobel Prize winners, reshaped the laws of physics and discovered the building blocks of life. UC Berkeley is a place where the extraordinary emerges from the everyday — and now that you’re here, you’re part of it. The legacy of our university is yours to continue. That’s what it means to be No. 1.

posts_meta.feature_image_alt: Launching in January 2027, the Nancy Pelosi Institute at UC Berkeley will encompass faculty research, teaching undergraduate courses and a visiting fellows program within the campus’s politic
The California state budget approved by Gov. Gavin Newsom reduces funding for the Middle Class Scholarship, aimed at low- to middle-income UC and CSU students.
Between 2019 and spring 2026, A’s at UC Berkeley consistently has stayed between 30% and 35%, with a dip during the COVID-19 pandemic likely due to increased pass/no pass courses.
UC undergraduate alumni break even on their educational investment approximately six years after initial enrollment, according to UC system data.
Despite facing cuts to the city of Berkeley’s Small Sites Program, community members successfully advocated for additional affordable housing funds.
Part of what made Berkeley the ideal setting for the shop was the city’s deep love for and appreciation for all kinds of food, according to a statement from Serotonin Scoops.
The Austria vs. Jordan game for the 2026 World Cup took place at San Francisco Bay Area Stadium on June 16.
